walkthrough.title: Split Into Two Teams
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Decrypto set up! First, split everyone into two roughly equal teams. You want at least two people on each team, though three or four per team is the sweet spot. Teams sit across from each other so they can see each other's clue screens. If you have an odd number, the smaller team goes first each round to compensate slightly.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Screens
walkthrough.text: Each team gets one screen. This is the big plastic stand-up device with four numbered slots on the team's side. The screen hides your team's keywords from the opposing team. Stand it up between the teams so only your side can see the keyword slots. The other team should never see what is behind your screen.

walkthrough.title: Draw Keyword Cards
walkthrough.text: Now each team draws four random keyword cards from the deck. These are the white cards with words printed on them. Slide one card into each of the four numbered slots on your screen, so you can see them labeled 1, 2, 3, and 4. These four words are your team's secret keywords for the entire game. Do not change them. Make sure everyone on your team can see them and memorize which number goes with which word.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Note Sheets
walkthrough.text: Give each team a note sheet. This is critical. The note sheet has columns for tracking clues given each round — both your own team's clues and the opposing team's clues. You will use this to build a picture of the other team's keywords over time. Grab a pen or pencil for each team too. Good note-taking wins games in Decrypto.

walkthrough.title: Place the Tokens and Code Cards
walkthrough.text: Put the interception tokens and miscommunication tokens in the center of the table where both teams can see them. These are how you win or lose. Also, shuffle the code cards into a face-down deck in the center. Each code card shows a three-digit combination using the numbers 1 through 4, like 2-4-1 or 3-1-2. You will draw one each round.

walkthrough.title: Choose Starting Encryptors
walkthrough.text: Each team picks one person to be the Encryptor for the first round. The Encryptor is the person who draws the secret code and writes clues for their team. This role rotates each round so everyone gets a turn. The Encryptor draws a code card, keeps it hidden, and will write three clues — one for each digit in the code. Ready? Let's learn the rules!

Keywords stay fixed for the entire game — memorize them

summary.title: Hand Out Note Sheets

Track your own clues AND the opposing team's clues each round

summary.title: Place the Tokens and Code Cards
bullets: Place interception tokens and miscommunication tokens centrally
Shuffle code cards into a face-down deck

summary.title: Choose Starting Encryptors
bullets: Each team picks 1 Encryptor for round 1
Encryptor role rotates each round
Encryptor draws code card and writes 3 clues (1 per digit)

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Decrypto is a team-based word game played over multiple rounds. Each team has four secret keywords numbered 1 through 4, hidden behind a screen. Every round, one player on each team — the Encryptor — draws a secret three-digit code and gives clues pointing to those numbered keywords. Your own team needs to decode your clues correctly, but the opposing team is also listening and trying to intercept your code. Two successful interceptions and the opposing team wins. Two miscommunications where your own team gets it wrong and you lose. The tension is beautiful: your clues must be clear enough for your team but cryptic enough to fool the opponents.

walkthrough.title: The Encryptor's Job
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, each team's Encryptor draws a code card and keeps it hidden from everyone else, including their own teammates. The code card shows three numbers, each between 1 and 4. For example, the code might be 3-1-4. The Encryptor then writes three clues on a piece of paper — one clue for each digit. Clue 1 relates to keyword number 3, clue 2 relates to keyword number 1, and clue 3 relates to keyword number 4. The clues can be a single word, a phrase, or a short sentence. They need to point your teammates toward the right keyword without being so obvious that the other team figures it out.

walkthrough.title: Clue Rules and Restrictions
walkthrough.text: There are some important rules about what clues you can give. You cannot use any form of the keyword itself, including translations, rhymes, or spelling tricks. You cannot give clues based on the position of the word on the screen, like saying 'the top one.' You cannot reference the number of letters in the word. You cannot use inside jokes or private references that only one teammate would know — clues must be based on publicly available knowledge. And you must keep clues brief. The key is creativity: find associations that your team will recognize from context but that sound ambiguous to the other team.

walkthrough.title: Guessing Phase — Own Team First
walkthrough.text: Once both Encryptors have written their clues, they read them aloud one at a time — clue 1, clue 2, clue 3. Now the Encryptor's own teammates discuss and try to figure out the three-digit code. They know the four keywords, so they are trying to match each clue to a keyword number. The team writes down their guess as a three-digit code. The key is that they have seen all past clues too, so they have context. In round 1, it is usually pretty straightforward because there is no history yet.

walkthrough.title: Interception Phase — Opposing Team Guesses
walkthrough.text: Here is where it gets spicy. After the own team guesses, the opposing team gets a chance to intercept. They have been listening to every clue given in every round and writing them down on their note sheet. Over time, they are building a model of what each keyword might be. They do not need to know the exact keywords — they just need to figure out which number each clue points to. The opposing team writes down their interception guess as a three-digit code. Important: there is no interception attempt in round 1. Opponents start intercepting from round 2 onward, once they have some clue history to work with.

walkthrough.title: Revealing the Code
walkthrough.text: The Encryptor now reveals the actual code card. First, check the own team's guess. If they got it wrong — meaning any digit is incorrect — the team receives a miscommunication token. That is bad. Two miscommunication tokens and your team loses immediately. Next, check the opposing team's interception guess. If they got all three digits correct, they earn an interception token. Two interception tokens and the intercepting team wins immediately. If neither win condition is met, play continues to the next round.

walkthrough.title: Recording Clues on the Note Sheet
walkthrough.text: After the code is revealed, both teams should update their note sheets. Write down all three clues from this round along with which keyword number each clue was actually pointing to. This is crucial for the opposing team because it lets them start mapping clue patterns to keyword numbers. For example, if keyword 2 has received clues like 'ocean,' 'waves,' and 'surfboard' over three rounds, the opposing team can guess that keyword 2 is probably something water-related. The note sheet is your weapon — use it.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: The game can end in three ways. First, if a team earns two interception tokens, that team wins. They have cracked the other team's code. Second, if a team earns two miscommunication tokens, that team loses. Their own teammates could not decode their clues. Third, if the code card deck runs out, the team with more interception tokens wins. If tied on interceptions, the team with fewer miscommunication tokens wins. If still tied, it is a draw. Most games end by interception or miscommunication well before the deck runs out.

bullets: Two teams, each with 4 secret keywords (numbered 1-4) behind a screen
Each round: Encryptor draws a 3-digit code, writes 3 clues
Own team decodes clues → opposing team tries to intercept
2 interceptions = win, 2 miscommunications = lose

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Game
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Decrypto! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through three rounds with two example teams — Team Blue and Team Red. You will see how clues work, how interception builds over time, and what happens when someone makes a critical mistake. Follow along with your own teams and try giving clues as we go. Ready? Let's crack some codes.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Keywords
walkthrough.text: Let's say Team Blue's four keywords behind their screen are: 1-PIANO, 2-OCEAN, 3-ROCKET, 4-SANDWICH. Team Red's keywords are: 1-CASTLE, 2-DIAMOND, 3-FOREST, 4-THUNDER. Remember, each team can only see their own keywords. The opposing team has no idea what these words are — yet. That is about to change.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 — Team Blue's Encryptor
walkthrough.text: Team Blue's Encryptor draws a code card: 2-4-1. That means clue 1 must point to keyword 2 (OCEAN), clue 2 must point to keyword 4 (SANDWICH), and clue 3 must point to keyword 1 (PIANO). The Encryptor writes: Clue 1: 'Waves.' Clue 2: 'Lunch.' Clue 3: 'Keys.' Simple, direct, and easy for the team to decode. In round 1, this is fine because the opponents have zero history to work with.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 — Team Blue Decodes
walkthrough.text: Team Blue's teammates hear 'Waves, Lunch, Keys.' They know their keywords. Waves clearly points to OCEAN, which is number 2. Lunch points to SANDWICH, which is number 4. Keys points to PIANO, which is number 1. They write down 2-4-1. The Encryptor reveals the code: 2-4-1. Perfect match! No miscommunication. And remember — no interception attempt in round 1, so Team Red just listens and takes notes.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 — Note Sheet Update
walkthrough.text: Now both teams update their note sheets. Team Red writes down: 'Blue's clues this round — Clue for position 1: Waves (keyword 2), Clue for position 2: Lunch (keyword 4), Clue for position 3: Keys (keyword 1).' Team Red now knows that Blue's keyword 2 got the clue 'Waves,' keyword 4 got 'Lunch,' and keyword 1 got 'Keys.' This is the foundation of their interception strategy. Every round adds more data.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 — Team Red's Turn
walkthrough.text: Now Team Red's Encryptor draws a code card: 3-1-2. Clue 1 points to keyword 3 (FOREST), clue 2 to keyword 1 (CASTLE), clue 3 to keyword 2 (DIAMOND). The Encryptor writes: Clue 1: 'Trees.' Clue 2: 'King.' Clue 3: 'Ring.' Team Red's teammates decode easily: Trees is FOREST (3), King is CASTLE (1), Ring is DIAMOND (2). Code: 3-1-2. Correct! Team Blue writes down Red's clues on their note sheet.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — The Clue Dilemma Begins
walkthrough.text: Round 2 is where the game gets interesting. New Encryptors take over. Team Blue's Encryptor draws 1-3-2. Now here is the challenge: last round, keyword 1 (PIANO) got the clue 'Keys.' If this round's Encryptor also writes something obvious like 'Music,' the opposing team will have two direct clues pointing at keyword 1. They might start to narrow it down. The Encryptor needs to be more creative this time.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Team Blue's Clues
walkthrough.text: Team Blue's Encryptor writes: Clue 1: 'Recital' (for PIANO, keyword 1). Clue 2: 'Launch' (for ROCKET, keyword 3). Clue 3: 'Tide' (for OCEAN, keyword 2). These are still fairly direct. 'Recital' connects to PIANO. 'Launch' connects to ROCKET. 'Tide' connects to OCEAN. The team decodes correctly: 1-3-2. But Team Red is paying close attention.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Team Red Attempts Interception
walkthrough.text: Team Red checks their note sheet. Last round, Blue's keyword 2 got 'Waves.' This round, one of the clues is 'Tide.' That is suspiciously water-related. They are pretty confident that 'Tide' points to whatever keyword 'Waves' pointed to — keyword 2. They also notice 'Recital' could be musical, and last round keyword 1 got 'Keys,' which could be piano keys. They guess: 1-3-2. The Encryptor reveals the code: 1-3-2. Team Red got it right! They earn an interception token! One more and Team Red wins.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake — Too-Obvious Clues
walkthrough.text: What just happened? Team Blue gave clues that were too directly connected to their keywords. 'Waves' and 'Tide' both scream water. 'Keys' and 'Recital' both scream music. By round 2, Team Red already had a strong map of Blue's keywords. This is the most common beginner mistake in Decrypto: giving the most obvious association every time. Your opponents are smart — they will connect the dots fast.

walkthrough.title: How to Fix It — Lateral Clues
walkthrough.text: The fix is to think laterally. Instead of 'Waves' and 'Tide' for OCEAN, try something unexpected. 'Pacific' works — it is an ocean, but it is also a movie, a region, and a word meaning peaceful. 'Salt' works — oceans are salty, but salt is also cooking, roads in winter, or a basketball term. The goal is to give a clue your team can decode in context, but that sounds like it could point to any of the four keywords from the outside. The best Encryptors make every clue feel ambiguous to the other team.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 — Team Blue Adjusts
walkthrough.text: Team Blue is now down one interception token. They need to be more careful. The new Encryptor draws 4-2-3. For keyword 4 (SANDWICH), instead of 'Lunch' again, they write 'Club.' For keyword 2 (OCEAN), instead of anything water-related, they write 'Floor.' For keyword 3 (ROCKET), they write 'Pocket.' Wait — 'Pocket rocket' is a common phrase, and 'Floor' as in ocean floor is pretty transparent. Let's see what happens.

walkthrough.title: Round 3 — The Miscommunication
walkthrough.text: Team Blue's teammates hear 'Club, Floor, Pocket.' They debate: is 'Club' referring to SANDWICH (club sandwich) or PIANO (jazz club)? 'Floor' — is that OCEAN (ocean floor) or PIANO (floor piano from the movie Big)? 'Pocket' — ROCKET (pocket rocket) or SANDWICH (pocket pita)? The team is confused. They guess 1-4-3, thinking 'Club' is jazz club (PIANO, keyword 1). But the actual code is 4-2-3. They got digit 1 wrong. Miscommunication token! Team Blue tried to get clever but swung too far in the other direction.

walkthrough.title: The Lesson — Balance Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Team Blue panicked after being intercepted and overcorrected. Their clues became so vague that even their own team could not decode them. This is the core tension of Decrypto: too obvious and you get intercepted, too obscure and you miscommunicate. The sweet spot is clues that have multiple possible meanings to outsiders but one clear meaning when you know the keyword. 'Club' for SANDWICH works if your team has never heard 'Club' used for PIANO. But if it is ambiguous to your own team, it is too risky.

walkthrough.title: Note Sheet Neglect
walkthrough.text: Here is another common mistake we saw in this practice game. Team Blue barely updated their note sheet for Team Red's clues. They were so focused on their own encoding problems that they forgot to track the other side. Meanwhile, Team Red was meticulously recording every Blue clue. That note sheet advantage is exactly why Red intercepted so quickly. Always keep your note sheet updated for both teams. Even if you are not the Encryptor this round, you should be writing things down.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues with new Encryptors each round. The game keeps escalating because both teams accumulate more clue data. By round 4 or 5, the opposing team might have three or four clues mapped to each keyword number. Your Encryptor needs to find clues that are increasingly creative. When all eight cubes are used, round over — but in Decrypto, rounds keep going until someone hits two tokens or the deck runs out.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations — you have just walked through the first three rounds of Decrypto! You have seen how clues work, watched Team Blue get intercepted for being too obvious, then miscommunicate for being too vague. You have learned the importance of note sheet discipline and lateral thinking. The sweet spot is in the middle: creative clues that your team gets but the opponents cannot lock down. You are ready to play for real!

walkthrough.title: Before You Go
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for clue-giving strategies and common traps to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all game terms and components.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun cracking codes!

summary.title: Before You Go
bullets: Check out General Tips → clue-giving strategies and common traps
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for all game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the biggest traps new players fall into. First, giving the most obvious word association every single round — this hands the other team an interception on a silver platter. Second, overcorrecting by giving clues so obscure that your own team cannot decode them. Third, neglecting the note sheet and losing track of the other team's clue patterns. Fourth, not discussing clue strategy with your team between rounds. And fifth, panicking after receiving one token and completely changing your approach. Stay calm, stay balanced, and keep communicating.

walkthrough.title: The Clue Spectrum
walkthrough.text: Think of every clue as falling on a spectrum from obvious to obscure. On the obvious end, you have direct synonyms and category words — 'Music' for PIANO, 'Water' for OCEAN. These are easy for your team but also easy for opponents to map over time. On the obscure end, you have loose associations and wordplay — 'Baby' for PIANO (baby grand) or 'Pacific' for OCEAN (could mean peaceful). The sweet spot is slightly toward the creative end: clues that your team can decode using shared context but that could plausibly point to multiple keywords from the outside.

walkthrough.title: Vary Your Clue Categories
walkthrough.text: One of the best habits you can build is varying the type of association you use for each keyword. If keyword 3 is ROCKET, do not always use space-themed clues. Round 1 might be 'Launch' (space). Round 2 could be 'Arugula' (rocket is a salad green in British English). Round 3 could be 'Bottle' (bottle rocket). Round 4 could be 'Skyrocket' — wait, that contains the word, so it is illegal. The point is, if each clue comes from a different angle, the other team cannot build a clean pattern. They will see 'Launch, Arugula, Bottle' and have no idea those all point to the same keyword.

walkthrough.title: Master the Note Sheet
walkthrough.text: Your note sheet is the most important tool in the game. After every round, record the opposing team's clues and which keyword number each clue pointed to. Over time, you are building a profile for each keyword. By round 3 or 4, you might see that their keyword 1 has received clues like 'Crown,' 'Fortress,' and 'Moat.' Even without knowing the exact word, you can guess that keyword 1 relates to royalty or medieval architecture. That is enough to intercept. Dedicate one person on your team to note-keeping if possible.

walkthrough.title: Read the Other Team's Body Language
walkthrough.text: Decrypto is a social game, and body language is fair game. Watch the other team when their Encryptor reads clues. If the guessers immediately nod and smile, the clue was probably very direct — that is a data point for your interception. If they look confused and start debating, the clue is more creative. Also watch the Encryptor's reaction when the team guesses. A visible wince usually means the team is wrong or debating the wrong keyword. Use this information to weight your interception guesses.

walkthrough.title: Communicate with Your Team
walkthrough.text: Between rounds, talk to your teammates about your keyword associations. You do not need to reveal your exact clue plan, but you can say things like 'I might go in an unusual direction for keyword 2 this round — think broadly.' This primes your team to consider less obvious connections. Some teams develop shared strategies, like always giving food-related clues for keyword 4 in the early rounds, then pivoting to something completely different once opponents catch on. Communication turns your team into a well-oiled code machine.

walkthrough.title: The Round 1 Trap
walkthrough.text: Round 1 feels safe because there is no interception. Many players use this as an excuse to give the most obvious clues possible. But here is the problem: those obvious clues become permanent data on the other team's note sheet. If you give 'Music' for PIANO in round 1, the opposing team now has a strong anchor for keyword 1. Every future clue for PIANO will be compared against 'Music,' and musical clues will be instantly recognized. Instead, use round 1 to establish a slightly misleading baseline. Give clues that are clear to your team but from unexpected angles.

walkthrough.title: Stay Calm After a Token
walkthrough.text: Getting an interception or miscommunication token can be rattling, but the worst thing you can do is panic. If you got intercepted, do not suddenly make all your clues impossibly obscure — that is how you earn a miscommunication token and potentially lose. If you earned a miscommunication, do not swing back to obvious clues — that invites interception. Instead, adjust incrementally. Move one step on the spectrum, not five. Talk to your team about what went wrong and refine, do not reinvent. The best teams treat tokens as feedback, not emergencies.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't repeat the same type of obvious association every round
Don't overcorrect with clues so vague your team can't decode
Don't neglect the note sheet — track both teams' clues
Don't panic after earning a token — adjust incrementally

summary.title: The Clue Spectrum
bullets: Obvious end: direct synonyms, category words (easy but interceptable)
Obscure end: loose associations, wordplay (risky for miscommunication)
Sweet spot: slightly creative, clear in context, ambiguous from outside
Test: would opponents confidently map this clue? If yes, too obvious

summary.title: Vary Your Clue Categories
bullets: Use different angles for the same keyword each round
Example for ROCKET: 'Launch' (space), 'Arugula' (salad), 'Bottle' (firework)
Mixed categories prevent opponents from building a clean pattern
Your team can decode from context; opponents see random noise

summary.title: Master the Note Sheet
bullets: Record opposing clues and keyword numbers every round
Build keyword profiles: 'Crown + Fortress + Moat' = royalty/medieval
You don't need exact keywords to intercept — patterns are enough
Dedicate one teammate to note-keeping

summary.title: Read the Other Team's Body Language
bullets: Immediate nods = direct clue (useful interception data)
Confusion and debate = creative/vague clue
Watch the Encryptor's reaction to teammate guesses
Body language is fair game — use it to weight interception guesses

summary.title: Communicate with Your Team
bullets: Warn teammates before giving unusual clues: 'think broadly for keyword 2'
Develop shared strategies: pivot clue styles once opponents catch on
You don't reveal exact plans, just prime teammates for creative thinking
Team communication turns good players into great teams

summary.title: The Round 1 Trap
bullets: Round 1 obvious clues become permanent data on opponent's note sheet
'Music' for PIANO in round 1 anchors keyword 1 forever
Use round 1 to set a slightly misleading baseline instead
Clear to your team, but from an unexpected angle

summary.title: Stay Calm After a Token
bullets: Intercepted? Don't make all clues impossibly obscure
Miscommunicated? Don't swing back to obvious clues
Adjust one step on the spectrum, not five
Treat tokens as feedback, not emergencies

walkthrough.title: The Decoy Clue Strategy
walkthrough.text: Advanced Encryptors deliberately craft clues that could plausibly point to multiple keywords. If your keywords are PIANO, OCEAN, ROCKET, and SANDWICH, the clue 'Keys' for PIANO is dangerous because it is uniquely musical. But 'Black' for PIANO works better — it could refer to black piano keys, but also black holes (ROCKET), black sand beaches (OCEAN), or a Black Forest sandwich. When the opposing team hears 'Black,' they cannot confidently assign it to any single keyword. This is the decoy strategy: every clue should feel like it could be for at least two of your keywords.

walkthrough.title: Keyword Overlap Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Study your four keywords for natural overlap zones. If you have OCEAN and SANDWICH, the word 'Sub' could reference a submarine (ocean) or a sub sandwich. This overlap is gold. Deliberately give clues that sit in the overlap between two keywords. Your team knows which keyword you mean from the code position, but the opponents see a clue that fits two possible mappings. Over multiple rounds, this overlap pollution makes their note sheet unreliable, because the same clue theme appears under different keyword numbers.

walkthrough.title: The Misdirection Baseline
walkthrough.text: In round 1, when there is no interception, you can set a deliberate misdirection. Give a clue for keyword 1 that sounds like it belongs to keyword 3. For example, if keyword 1 is PIANO and keyword 3 is ROCKET, give 'Space bar' for PIANO — your team knows the space bar is on a keyboard (near piano keys), but the opponent will likely file it under their guess for the space-themed keyword. This poisons their note sheet from round 1. When they later try to intercept, their model will be built on a flawed foundation.

walkthrough.title: Interception Pattern Recognition
walkthrough.text: To intercept effectively, look for convergence in the opposing team's clue history. If keyword 2 has received 'Waves,' 'Tide,' and 'Coral' over three rounds, the pattern is clearly aquatic. But advanced opponents will try to break patterns. Watch for the break: if keyword 2 suddenly gets a clue like 'Blue,' it might still be aquatic (blue ocean) or it might be the Encryptor pivoting to a different association (blue as a color, emotion, or music genre). Cross-reference with body language and the guess difficulty — if the team guessed quickly, the clue is probably still on theme.

walkthrough.title: The Sacrifice Interception
walkthrough.text: When your team already has one interception token, the opponents will be desperate. They know one more interception means they lose. This desperation causes them to give extremely vague clues, which increases their miscommunication risk. You can exploit this by pressuring them through confident interception attempts, even if you are not 100 percent sure. Announce your interception guess with confidence. The psychological pressure alone can cause the other team's Encryptor to panic and make their clues even more obscure next round, further increasing their miscommunication odds.

walkthrough.title: Encryptor Role Preparation
walkthrough.text: Before your turn as Encryptor, pre-plan clue options for every possible three-digit code. There are 24 possible codes (4 choose 3, times the orderings). For each keyword, have three or four backup clue ideas ready in your head, each from a different category of association. When you draw the code card, you can immediately pull from your prepared list instead of thinking on the spot. This reduces the time pressure and prevents you from defaulting to obvious clues. Great Encryptors are prepared Encryptors.

walkthrough.title: Team Signaling Through Clue Order
walkthrough.text: Experienced teams develop subtle signaling systems. While you cannot give position-based clues about the keyword screen, you can develop conventions about clue style. For example, your team might agree that abstract clues always indicate a less obvious association. Or that two-word clue phrases signal a compound-word connection. These meta-agreements are perfectly legal as long as they are about clue interpretation style, not about encoding hidden information in the clue itself. Just make sure your whole team is on the same page before the game starts.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Survival
walkthrough.text: In the late game, both teams have extensive note sheet data, and interception becomes much easier. If you are in a survival position — one token already on the board — your Encryptor needs to use maximum creativity. Consider cultural references, puns, homonyms, and multi-language associations. The clue 'Major' for PIANO (C major) is strong because 'Major' has so many meanings: military rank, college major, major league, and more. In late-game situations, ambiguity is your shield. But never forget: your team still needs to decode correctly. One miscommunication ends the game.

summary.title: The Decoy Clue Strategy
bullets: Craft clues that could plausibly point to 2+ keywords
'Black' for PIANO also fits ROCKET (black hole), OCEAN (black sand), etc.
Opponents cannot confidently assign decoy clues to one keyword
Goal: every clue should feel ambiguous from the outside

summary.title: Keyword Overlap Exploitation
bullets: Find natural overlap zones between your 4 keywords
'Sub' works for OCEAN (submarine) and SANDWICH (sub sandwich)
Overlap clues pollute opponents' note sheet across multiple keywords
Your team knows the answer from code position; opponents see confusion

summary.title: The Misdirection Baseline
bullets: Round 1: give clues that opponents will misfile on their note sheet
'Space bar' for PIANO — team gets keyboard; opponents think space/ROCKET
Poisons opponent's data model from the very first round
Their future interception attempts are built on a flawed foundation

summary.title: Interception Pattern Recognition
bullets: Look for convergence: 'Waves + Tide + Coral' = aquatic keyword
Watch for pattern breaks when opponents pivot clue styles
Cross-reference with body language and decode difficulty
Quick teammate decode = clue is still on theme

summary.title: The Sacrifice Interception
bullets: With 1 interception token, opponents get desperate and give vague clues
Desperate vague clues increase miscommunication risk
Announce interception guesses with confidence to amplify pressure
Psychological pressure alone can cause Encryptor to panic

summary.title: Encryptor Role Preparation
bullets: Pre-plan 3-4 backup clue ideas per keyword before your turn
Use different association categories for each backup clue
When you draw the code, pull from your prepared list instantly
Preparation prevents defaulting to obvious clues under time pressure

summary.title: Team Signaling Through Clue Order
bullets: Develop team conventions about clue style (abstract = less obvious link)
Two-word phrases might signal compound-word connections
Meta-agreements about interpretation style are legal
Agree on conventions before the game starts

summary.title: Late-Game Survival
bullets: Late game: extensive note sheet data makes interception easier
Use puns, homonyms, multi-language associations, cultural references
'Major' for PIANO (C major) has many alternative meanings
Ambiguity is your shield — but your team must still decode correctly

entries.definition: The four secret words hidden behind each team's screen, numbered 1 through 4. Keywords are drawn randomly at the start of the game and remain fixed for the entire game. Each team can only see their own keywords. The opposing team tries to deduce them through accumulated clue analysis.

Cards showing a three-digit combination using the numbers 1 through 4, such as 2-4-1 or 3-1-2. Each round, the Encryptor draws one code card secretly and writes three clues corresponding to the three digits. The code card deck is shuffled and placed face-down in the center of the table.

The player on each team who draws the secret code card and writes three clues for their teammates. The Encryptor role rotates each round so every player gets a turn. The Encryptor cannot help their team guess — they just read the clues aloud and wait.

When the opposing team correctly guesses all three digits of your code. Earned by analyzing accumulated clue history on the note sheet to map clues to keyword numbers. Two successful interceptions wins the game for the intercepting team. No interception attempts are allowed in round 1.

When your own team fails to correctly guess your three-digit code. This means at least one digit was wrong. Earning a miscommunication token is bad — two miscommunication tokens means your team loses immediately. Miscommunications usually result from overly vague or ambiguous clues.

A tracking sheet where teams record all clues given each round and which keyword number each clue pointed to. Used to build a model of the opposing team's keywords over time. Good note-keeping is essential for successful interception. Both your own and the opposing team's clues should be tracked.

The stand-up device that hides each team's four keyword cards from the opposing team. The screen has four numbered slots (1-4) where keyword cards are inserted. Only the team sitting behind the screen can see their keywords. The screen stays in place for the entire game.

Restrictions on what clues the Encryptor can give. No using any form of the keyword itself (including translations, rhymes, or spelling hints). No position-based references. No letter-count hints. No private references or inside jokes. Clues must be based on publicly available knowledge and kept brief.

In the first round of the game, the opposing team does not attempt an interception. This gives both teams one round to establish clues before opponents begin analyzing patterns. Starting from round 2, both teams attempt interceptions every round.

An optional sand timer included in the game box. It can be used to limit how long the Encryptor has to write clues or how long teams have to discuss and guess. Using the timer is optional but recommended for keeping the game moving at a fun pace, especially with larger groups.

A token awarded to a team when they successfully intercept the opposing team's code (correctly guess all three digits). Placed visibly on the table. A team that earns two interception tokens wins the game immediately.

A token given to a team when their own teammates fail to decode the Encryptor's clues correctly (any digit wrong). Placed visibly on the table. A team that earns two miscommunication tokens loses the game immediately.
